author: @antoniopacelli
text:
Hi Sir !
I noticed you are pretty skilled and informed about Graphite, generator and magnets.
Can you help me with this: 
Think at an optimized version of the low heat Stirling engine that by now it's all over the places.
(Just google low heat Stirling engine it's the circular one)
Now think at it in his bigger ~25/30kw of mechanical power production version.
Question is:
How would you creating a cost effective Generator from the Iron Nitride magnets and Graphite (and Graphite sheets) that would be left over from the motor production.
You have a 500$ per unit price limit..
And likely a power production ranging from 20 to 25kw ...

I'm asking you because I can't really wrapping my brain around circuits...
But I figured that some magnets and highly conductive materials disks turning in different directions at high speed would generating current..
The Stirling and some multipliers mechanism would give the spin to the different sections/layers spinning around.. but I cannot really get to the material part of the problem.
 I have of no possibility of getting anything about currents fluxes that aren't organically produced..

Sincerely.
Researchers for passion.
[EDIT]
Ok like this is too much generic..!
For better specifying my example:
A ring of Iron Nitride with above and below disks of Graphite with many fissure orbiting around in opposite direction to each other, than another layers on top, four times for four piston activated from the Stirling motions. 
Connected to some brushes for any plate..or one bigger to brush them all..."The brush of Power."
(Pardon my Tolkienism, I just rediscovered brush existence)
Also for reducing the Iron Nitride usage and total weight and costs, 
In between the 2 sections 
(of 8 plates in total) could be just added a steel plate so that just one magnet above and one below would be needed , thanks to the redirected electromagnetic field attracted from the steel plate? (strongly fixated [ The only non conductive material that pops into my brain as light,  cheep and inexpensive is wood...looking fragile, but those plastic bee hive superstrong materials must be so much expensive, they were thought as wheels for armoured vehicles] in a equidistant positioning in order to not being itself catched from the high strength of the magnets)
Costs for iron nitride would not being a problem because it become cheaper producing it in great quantity (for the nitrogen)

Is the thing I described above a generator?
It would produce at least 20Kw per Stirling unit with mechanical power production of ~25/30kw?
Thanks for yours attention.

P.S: Yours impersonation of Antony Hopkins must be Gold.

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text:
In principle your idea should work. It seems you are thinking of a homo-polar generator, am I right? But you would need some way of keeping the hot/cold components from interfering with heat flow whatever you do. You don't need FeN or 'graphitic' materials, it's more about coupling ratios, and there are plenty of other materials. Try a few small-scale experiments. Scaling up to 20kw, or above, could require some major redesigning too.
A 'pancake' type motor/generator set up might be worth considering, but for testing any 'ordinary' generator will do.
Apologies, but I don't understand,  "I have of no possibility of getting anything about currents fluxes that aren't organically produced.." Please explain.
Obviously English is not your first language (you've done very well though), could you get someone to explain your idea more formally? Perhaps someone could then answer you directly.
Good luck.

author: @timothygorman7528
text: This is going to sound silly ,but, having a Marching Band background (I was the awesomely cool Tuba Player), this could be part a cool effect with the band front, as most performances happen at night... To use one of these, build it so that it's wired back to the end (where the ball is), and have it be some sort of LED lit "ball" on the end.   They could swing them around and get a cool LED circle effect as part of "the show," and could be themed to technology, or the future, or "saving the environment" by not using batteries, etc.  Pretty cool really.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: mate - that is more than pretty cool - I like it - a lot!

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text: These, as you describe, have been on the market for decades. I first saw one as a skipping rope with LEDs in a clear vinyl tube. Plenty other examples since, some even leave images in the air due to persistence of vision effects. Or fixed to bicycle spokes and fan-blades as advertising signs etc.

author: @markg1051
text:
Can you do some actual voltage and current measurements with a known load resistance across the motor terminals?
If you are correct about rpm per volt (not saying that you are not!😎), you could use the idea as an rpm measuring device.
Lighting LEDs is fine as a visual for the entertainment.
Cheers.

author: @ryanjamesloyd6733
text:
wonderful!  
Similar to a notion I've had, but don't know how to do, quite.  if you put that on top of a bit of a rod, and move that rod back and forth Very slightly, it'll spin beautifully. 
It seems to me, as it's gravity and centripital(fugal?) force that keeps it spinning and you get some flywheel type action going, and the rod is taking all the weight,  that you could use a very much smaller motor to jiggle the rod, and wind up with more output from the motor cranked by the spinning arm, than is required to jiggle the rod.     
does that make sense?
the concept could scale up by quite a lot, I've just not figured out how to make a rod-jiggler.

replies.author: @ryanjamesloyd6733
replies.text: I mean, depending on the length of the arm, and amount of weight, you could turn quite a large motor, with a very minor nudge to the rod it's on, and get it going Very fast with hardly any effort.

author: @stevetobias4890
text: That's actually pretty decent when you think about it. Imagine about 6 or more of these attached to a large cog that is driven by your large wind blades. In light winds if you got about the same rpm as you just demonstrated you then should get about an amp at 20 volts or more. Add a buck converter that gives you approximately 12v @ 2amps or 5v @ a minimum of 4.5amps. Sure you will need a weatherproof housing for the motors but seriously this has real potential.
